title: Medigap Premiums on The Rise
rawContent:
Understanding Why Your Medigap Premiums Are Rising in 2026

Medicare supplement premium increases rarely happen in isolation. When Medicare's own costs climb, private insurers that sell Medigap policies adjust accordingly, and 2026 brought some of the sharpest underlying cost shifts in recent memory.

The 2026 Medicare Part B standard premium jumped to $202.90 per month, a 9.7% increase from $185 in 2025. Because many Medigap plans cover Part B coinsurance, insurers factor these rising base costs directly into their rate filings. The Part A hospital deductible also climbed to $1,736 in 2026, up from $1,676 the prior year, and plans designed to cover that deductible must price accordingly.

These aren't isolated cost spikes. Healthcare analysts consistently point to rising utilization rates and persistent medical inflation as the structural forces behind annual Medigap monthly premium adjustments. The increases reflect what beneficiaries are actually using, more procedures, more prescriptions, more specialist visits, pushing the total cost of coverage upward every year.

Key Takeaways

  • Medigap premiums are rising in 2026 largely because Medicare's own costs, including the Part B premium at $202.90 and the Part A deductible at $1,736, increased significantly.

  • Your policy's rating method (attained-age, issue-age, or community-rated) is the single biggest factor determining how much your premium will grow over time.

  • You have options beyond just accepting higher costs, including switching to High-Deductible Plan G, shopping competing carriers, or consulting a licensed Medicare agent to compare plans in your area.

Decoding Your Medigap Policy's Premium Structure: Attained-Age vs. Issue-Age vs. Community-Rated

Not all Medigap pricing methods work the same way. Understanding how your specific policy is rated tells you a great deal about what your premium trajectory will look like over the next decade.

Attained-age policies the most common type sold today, tie your premium directly to your current age. That means two separate cost pressures stack on top of each other: annual medical inflation adjustments and automatic increases as you grow older. Over a 10- or 15-year horizon, these compounding factors can make a seemingly affordable policy significantly more expensive.

Issue-age policies lock in your rating based on the age you were when you first enrolled. Premiums still rise over time due to inflation and claims trends, but your age itself no longer triggers automatic increases. These plans tend to offer more predictable costs for long-term planners.

Community-rated policies charge all enrollees the same premium regardless of age. A 68-year-old and an 80-year-old pay the same rate. This structure typically benefits older enrollees and those with health concerns, though younger beneficiaries often pay more than they would under age-based alternatives.

Navigating the Trade-Off: Medigap vs. Medicare Advantage in 2026

When Medigap premiums are rising, the idea of switching to a Medicare Advantage plan with a $0 monthly premium can feel appealing. Roughly 14 million Americans currently hold Medigap policies, and many are weighing exactly this question.

The honest answer is: it depends heavily on your situation. For frequent travelers, those managing complex or chronic conditions, or anyone who values unrestricted access to any Medicare-accepting provider nationwide, Medigap often remains the stronger financial choice, even at a higher monthly cost. A $0-premium Medicare Advantage plan with a $9,250 maximum out-of-pocket limit in 2026 can quickly become expensive if you need significant care.

Consider a scenario: a beneficiary with two hospitalizations in a year could face thousands in network-based cost-sharing under a Medicare Advantage plan, while Medigap Plan G would cover most of those costs after a single Part B deductible of $283.

The most critical risk in switching is often invisible until it's too late. In most states, once you leave Medigap for Medicare Advantage, you lose guaranteed-issue rights for Medigap. Returning later may require medical underwriting, and coverage could be denied based on your health history.

That exit ramp is not always available on the way back.

Smart Strategies to Manage Your Medigap Costs in 2026

Rising premiums don't mean you're out of options. Several practical strategies can help you manage your Medigap costs without sacrificing the coverage quality that matters most to your health and budget.

High-Deductible Plan G (HD Plan G) deserves serious attention for beneficiaries who are generally healthy and want lower monthly premiums. In 2026, HD Plan G carries a $2,950 deductible before benefits kick in, but the monthly premiums are substantially lower than standard Plan G. If you rarely use significant medical services in a given year, the math often favors HD Plan G, you pay less per month and may never reach the deductible. For those who do hit the deductible, standard Plan G may still offer better overall value.

Household and loyalty discounts are frequently overlooked. Many insurers offer 5%–12% discounts when two members of the same household both carry policies with that carrier. It's worth calling your insurer directly and asking, these discounts aren't always advertised prominently.

Make it a habit to review your Medigap coverage annually. Your health needs evolve, and what made sense at 66 may not be the best fit at 74. You can visit Medicare FAQ for general information to support your annual review process.

Pro Tip

Before your next Medigap renewal date, request rate increase history from your current insurer, specifically, ask for the percentage increase applied each of the last five years on your plan type. Then get competing quotes for the same plan letter from at least two other carriers in your state. Because Medigap plan benefits are standardized by federal law, the only real variable between a Plan G from Carrier A and Plan G from Carrier B is the premium and the company's financial stability rating.

Understanding Your Rights and Protections with Medigap

You have more protections as a Medigap policyholder than many people realize. Federal and state rules establish clear boundaries on what insurers can and cannot do.

Guaranteed-issue rights allow you to purchase a Medigap policy without medical underwriting in specific situations, most notably when you lose employer-sponsored coverage or when your Medicare Advantage plan leaves your area. Outside of these trigger events, insurers in most states can use health status to price or deny coverage.

State insurance departments regulate Medigap premium increases. Before any rate hike takes effect, insurers must file justifications with the state regulator. The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) provides model regulations that most states follow, creating a baseline of oversight. Some states have stronger consumer protections than others, so knowing your state's rules matters.

Medicare. gov's plan finder tool lets you compare Medigap premiums available in your ZIP code side by side. It's one of the most underused resources available to beneficiaries. Also, reviewing your Medicare Summary Notice regularly helps you verify that your claims are processed correctly and that you're not being billed in error, an important step in managing your overall healthcare spending.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  1. Dropping Medigap without understanding underwriting risk. Many beneficiaries leave Medigap for a lower-premium Medicare Advantage plan and later discover they cannot return to Medigap due to health conditions. In most states, insurers can deny Medigap applications outside guaranteed-issue windows. Always confirm your state's rules before dropping coverage.

  2. Comparing plans only by monthly premium. A lower monthly premium does not equal lower total spending. Factor in deductibles, copays, coinsurance, and your estimated annual utilization. Someone with frequent specialist visits could pay far more out-of-pocket on a low-premium plan than on a higher-premium one.

  3. Ignoring the pricing method of your policy. Attained-age policies increase automatically as you age, on top of inflation adjustments. If you didn't ask whether your policy is attained-age, issue-age, or community-rated when you enrolled, find out now, it directly predicts your future premium trajectory.

  4. Missing the Medigap Open Enrollment window. The six-month period that begins when you turn 65 and enroll in Part B is your strongest window for guaranteed coverage. Missing it means you may face medical underwriting for the rest of your life. Many people aren't aware this window closes and doesn't reopen on demand.

  5. Assuming all Medigap plans of the same letter are identical in price. Plan G is Plan G in terms of benefits, but premiums vary significantly between carriers for the exact same coverage. Not shopping multiple carriers is one of the most common and costly oversights, potentially leaving hundreds of dollars per year on the table.

  6. Overlooking state-specific assistance programs. Some states offer Medicare Savings Programs or other assistance for beneficiaries who struggle with premiums. Many eligible people never apply simply because they don't know these programs exist. Check with your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) to see what's available where you live.

Beyond Premiums: The True Cost of Your Medicare Choices

Focusing only on the monthly premium is one of the most common planning errors in Medicare. Your real annual cost is shaped by four components working together: your premium, your deductible, your copayments, and your coinsurance.

Understanding how each piece works is essential. A premium is what you pay each month regardless of whether you use care. A deductible is what you pay before coverage begins. A copay is a fixed dollar amount per visit or service. Coinsurance is a percentage of the cost you owe after your deductible.

Together, these determine your true out-of-pocket costs in any given year.

Medigap Plans K and L offer a different cost structure worth considering. In 2026, Plan K carries a maximum out-of-pocket limit of $8,000, while Plan L caps at $4,000. Both plans require more cost-sharing along the way but provide a ceiling on total exposure, and they typically come with lower monthly premiums than Plans F or G.

For beneficiaries living on fixed incomes, even modest annual premium increases compound into meaningful budget pressure over time. A holistic cost comparison, not just premium shopping, is the most financially sound approach to evaluating your Medicare choices each year.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medigap Premiums

Why are Medigap premiums going up every year?

Medigap premiums rise because the underlying costs of healthcare keep increasing. Insurers adjust rates to account for rising medical utilization, inflation in healthcare services, and annual increases in Medicare's own cost-sharing, including the Part B premium now at $202.90 in 2026. If you have an attained-age policy, your premium also increases automatically each year simply because you're a year older.

How much did Medigap premiums increase in 2026?

Specific Medigap plan rate hikes vary by insurer, plan type, and state. However, the Medicare cost drivers behind them were significant: the Part B standard premium rose 9.7% to $202.90, and the Part A deductible increased to $1,736. These underlying shifts push Medigap carriers to file for rate increases to maintain adequate reserves.

Can my Medigap insurance company raise my premium?

Yes, insurers can and do raise Medigap premiums annually. Increases reflect medical inflation, overall claims experience, and, for attained-age policies, your advancing age. All rate increases must be filed and reviewed by your state's insurance department before they take effect.

What is the average cost of a Medigap plan in 2026?

Medigap monthly premiums vary widely based on plan type, state, age, and insurer. Generally speaking, popular plans like G and F average roughly $150–$250 per month, translating to approximately $1,800–$3,000 annually. Your specific quote will differ based on your location and rating method. Exploring the

com/faqs/average-cost-of-medicare-supplement-plans/">average cost of Medicare supplement plans can give you a useful starting benchmark.

Should I switch from Medigap to Medicare Advantage because of rising premiums?

This is one of the most consequential decisions you can make in Medicare. Medicare Advantage plans often have lower monthly premiums, but they come with provider networks, prior authorization requirements, and out-of-pocket maximums up to $9,250 in 2026. More importantly, switching may permanently close your path back to Medigap in states that allow medical underwriting outside guaranteed-issue periods.

Which Medigap plan has the lowest premium in 2026?

High-Deductible Plan G typically carries the lowest monthly premium among standard Medigap options. The trade-off is a $2,950 deductible in 2026 before the plan begins paying benefits. Plans K and L also have lower premiums but involve cost-sharing up to their respective out-of-pocket maximums of $8,000 and $4,000.

What if I can no longer afford my Medigap premium?

Start by comparing HD Plan G, Plan K, or Plan L as lower-cost alternatives within Medigap itself. Also check whether you qualify for Medicare Savings Programs or state-specific assistance. You can find additional strategies at ways to reduce Medicare premiums to explore all available options before making any coverage changes.

Does Medigap cover Part B excess charges?

Plans F and G both cover Part B excess charges, the difference between what a provider bills and the Medicare-approved amount. This protection can be valuable if you see providers who do not accept Medicare assignment. For a full explanation, see the details on Part B excess charges and how they affect your billing.

Making Confident Medicare Decisions in a Changing Landscape

Medigap premiums are rising in 2026, and that trend is unlikely to reverse. But understanding why costs are increasing, and knowing what tools are available to manage them, puts you in a much stronger position than most beneficiaries.

Review your plan annually. Understand your policy's rating structure. Compare carriers for the same plan letter. And don't make the decision to leave Medigap without fully understanding what you may give up in the process.

If you want personalized guidance, speaking with a licensed Medicare agent who can compare multiple carriers in your area is one of the most practical steps you can take. The right coverage isn't just about the lowest premium today, it's about the best financial protection for the years ahead.
excerpt: Medigap premiums are rising in 2026 due to increased Medicare costs, including a jump in Part B premiums and the Part A deductible. Learn why your premiums are increasing and what you can do.

Options to manage rising costs include switching to High-Deductible Plan G, shopping for new carriers, or consulting a licensed Medicare agent.
Rising utilization rates and persistent medical inflation are structural forces driving annual Medigap premium adjustments.
Attained-age policies, the most common type, see premiums increase with both medical inflation and your age, leading to compounding cost pressures.
